The GDG identified important knowledge gaps directly related to the PICO questions, or which may have 
a direct impact on the implementation of these recommendations. The following areas were identified as 
priority questions for high-quality evidence generation.
5.1 Core practices in caring for women with diabetes 
during pregnancy
• What are women’s preferences and values regarding diabetes education during pregnancy, and how do 
these affect engagement and outcomes?
• What is the effectiveness of culturally sensitive, woman-centred education interventions in improving 
maternal and neonatal outcomes in women with diabetes?
• What is the appropriate gestational weight gain range for women with diabetes, and how does it differ from 
general pregnancy recommendations?
• What training or task-sharing models effectively support non-specialist providers to deliver diabetes care 
and education during pregnancy in low-resource settings?
• How can self-care be supported and which services and support can be provided via telehealth  
(e.g. antenatal education, check-ins to assess glycaemic control)?
• Can a risk prediction tool or scoring system be developed and validated to identify pregnant women with 
diabetes at higher risk of adverse outcomes?
• What is the availability and utility of epidemiological outcome data disaggregated by diabetes status  
(type 1, type 2, GDM) versus general ‘high-risk pregnancy’ categories?
5.2 Glucose monitoring
• What are the optimal glycaemic targets in pregnancy for women with type 1, type 2 diabetes, or GDM to 
balance maternal and neonatal outcomes while minimizing hypoglycaemia?
5 
Research 
implications

• How should HbA1c and CGM targets be defined and used for different types of diabetes during pregnancy, 
especially in type 2 diabetes and GDM?
• What is the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of CGM in pregnant women with type 2 or GDM, 
particularly women on insulin?
• How do serial HbA1c measurements and their trajectory across pregnancy relate to maternal and neonatal 
outcomes in women with type 1 or type 2 diabetes?
• What maternal and neonatal outcomes are associated with different glycaemic thresholds (e.g. fasting,  
1 hour, 2 hour) based on Hyperglycemia and Adverse Pregnancy Outcome study (HAPO)-derived odds 
ratios, and how should they inform diagnostic criteria?
5.3 Pharmacotherapy
5.3.1 Type 1 diabetes in pregnancy
• What is the effectiveness of different insulin types (short- and long-acting human and analogue, and 
premixed), regimens (twice daily and basal-bolus) and delivery methods (syringe and vials, pens, insulin 
pumps, automated insulin delivery systems) on glycaemic control and maternal and neonatal outcomes 
for women with type 1 diabetes?
• Does the addition of metformin to insulin improve glycaemic control and maternal and neonatal outcomes 
compared to insulin alone?
• Can automated insulin delivery systems, with algorithms tailored to the physiological needs of pregnancy, 
be safely, effectively, and equitably implemented in low- and middle-income countries?
• What is the cost-effectiveness, and what are the barriers to, and facilitators of, implementing advanced 
insulin technologies (pumps, automated insulin delivery systems) in low-resource settings?
• How does the availability of insulin and monitoring resources and the quality and accessibility of the care 
in low- and middle-income countries influence maternal and neonatal outcomes?
5.3.2 Type 2 diabetes in pregnancy
• How do metformin and insulin compare as first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes in terms of glycaemic 
control and maternal and neonatal outcomes?
• What is the effect of continuing metformin alone versus switching to insulin during pregnancy on glycaemic 
control and maternal and neonatal outcomes? 
• What is the effectiveness and safety of oral glucose-lowering agents during pregnancy in terms of 
glycaemic control and maternal and neonatal outcomes?
• How does combining metformin and insulin compare to monotherapy with either agent alone for women 
with type 2 diabetes?
• What are the impacts of in utero  exposure to metformin on child growth, adiposity, and metabolic 
outcomes compared with children of women with type 2 diabetes in pregnancy not exposed to metformin?
 Research implications
41

42
• How do twice-daily, basal bolus and premixed insulin regimens compare in terms of efficacy, safety, and 
feasibility in low-resource settings?
• What are the barriers, facilitators, and cost-effectiveness implications of scaling up insulin and metformin 
access, including advanced insulin delivery methods, in low- and middle-income countries?
5.3.3 Gestational diabetes mellitus
• How do metformin and insulin compare as first-line treatment for GDM in terms of glycaemic control and 
maternal and neonatal outcomes?
• How does combining metformin and insulin compare to monotherapy with either agent alone for women 
with GDM?
• Which women with GDM require escalation to insulin, and what criteria best predict treatment failure with 
metformin or diet alone?
• What are the impacts of in utero  exposure to metformin on child growth, adiposity, and metabolic 
outcomes compared with children of women with GDM in pregnancy not exposed to metformin?
• What are the short-term and long-term maternal and neonatal outcomes, acceptability, adherence, 
and cost-effectiveness of GDM treatments (metformin, insulin, combination) in low- and middle-income 
countries?
5.4 Additional monitoring and assessments
• For which subgroups of women with GDM is multidisciplinary or intensified care beneficial, and how should 
‘specialized care’ be defined?
• What is the comparative effectiveness of different fetal monitoring strategies (e.g. ultrasound, Doppler, 
cardiotocography, fundal height) in improving outcomes among pregnant women with diabetes?
• How acceptable and feasible are various fetal monitoring strategies for women with diabetes across 
different health system settings?
• What is the optimal timing and frequency of retinopathy screening during pregnancy for women with pre-
existing diabetes?
• Is serum creatinine or eGFR a more accurate marker for assessing renal function during pregnancy in 
women with diabetes?
WHO recommendations on care for women with diabetes during pregnancy

The Maternal and Perinatal Health Guideline Technical Advisory Group convenes regularly to review WHO’s 
current portfolio of maternal and perinatal health recommendations and to help WHO prioritize new and 
existing questions for recommendation development and updating. These recommendations will be included 
in those reviews. In the event that new evidence that could potentially impact the current evidence base 
is identified, these recommendations may be updated. If no new reports or information is identified, the 
recommendations may be revalidated.
WHO welcomes suggestions regarding additional questions for inclusion in the updated recommendations. 
Please email your suggestions to srhmph@who.int.

Priority outcomes included for all PICOs
Critical maternal outcomes 
• Maternal death
• Maternal functioning and well-being
• Women’s views and experiences 
Important maternal outcomes 
• Antenatal hospitalization
• Admission to intensive care
• Mode of birth
Critical newborn outcomes
• Stillbirth/fetal death
• Neonatal death
• Perinatal death
Important newborn outcomes
• Admission to neonatal intensive care
• Gestational age at birth
• Preterm birth (<32 weeks; <37 weeks)
• Birthweight
• Intrauterine growth restriction
• Low birthweight (<2500 g)
• Small-for-gestational age (<10th  centile for 
gestational age)
• Apgar score of less than seven at five minutes
• Neurodevelopmental outcomes (into infancy 
and childhood)
Health service outcomes
• Antenatal admissions to hospital for the woman 
and length of stay
• Emergency department visits for the woman
• Admission to intensive care unit for the woman
• Length of postnatal hospitalization for the 
woman
• Length of neonatal hospitalization 
• Costs of care for the woman or baby or both
Annex 2. 
Priority outcomes used in 
decision making

Priority outcomes for individual PICOs
Maternal outcomes 
• Early pregnancy loss  
(spontaneous or elective)
• Induction of labour
• Gestational weight change
• Glycaemia (time in range, hypoglycaemia)
• Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy (pre-
eclampsia; any hypertensive disorder of 
pregnancy)
• Postpartum haemorrhage
• Childbirth-related injuries (e.g.  perineal  
injuries etc)
• Diabetes later in life (maternal) 
• Diabetes in the longer term (for GDM population)
• Diabetes-specific patient-reported outcomes
• Chronic kidney disease
• Long-term cardiometabolic outcomes
• Obesity in the longer term
• Visual loss or blindness
• Worsening retinopathy
Newborn outcomes 
• Amniotic fluid abnormalities (polyhydramnios 
and oligohydramnios)
• Macrosomia
• Large-for-gestational age 
• Birth injury
• Shoulder dystocia 
• Neonatal hypoglycaemia
• Hyperbilirubinaemia 
• Respiratory distress syndrome 
• Transient tachypnoea of the newborn
• Neonatal seizures
• Congenital anomalies (among women who 
may have had poor glycaemic control prior to 
pregnancy) 
• Breastfeeding
• Long term cardiometabolic outcomes

iv 
 
Abbreviations and acronyms 
 
CVD cardiovascular disease 
DKA diabetic ketoacidosis 
GDC WHO Global Diabetes Compact 
IDF  International Diabetes Federation 
LMIC low- and middle-income country 
NCD noncommunicable disease 
TAG-D WHO Technical Advisory Group on Diabetes 
WHO World Health Organization

1 
 
Overview 
 
The World Health Organization (WHO) convened the eighth meeting of the WHO 
Technical Advisory Group on Diabetes (TAG-D) on 16 and 17 June 2025. TAG-D 
provides technical advice to WHO on matters relating to WHO’s work on 
diabetes. Eight TAG-D members were present on day one and nine were present 
on day two. Dr Nikhil Tandon chaired the meeting, with Dr Yacoba Atiase as 
rapporteur.  
 
All participants provided declarations of interests (DOIs); all DOIs were reviewed 
by WHO. Four TAG-D members declared interests that triggered further review. 
All declared interests were deemed unlikely to affect the experts’ judgment. 
 
The agenda and list of attendees can be found in Annex 1 and Annex 2, 
respectively.  
 
Summary of discussions 
 
The discussions of the eighth meeting of TAG-D were held over five sessions: 
 
1. update on the WHO Global Diabetes Compact; 
2. development of WHO recommendations on the management 
of dyslipidaemia for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular 
disease;  
3. demand forecasting tool for NCD medicines and health technologies; 
4. technical report on diabetes and oral diseases; and 
5. establishing the baseline for the global diabetes coverage targets. 
 
Session 1: Update on the WHO Global Diabetes 
Compact 
 
Dr Bianca Hemmingsen, Technical Lead for Diabetes, Department of 
Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs), Rehabilitation and Disability at WHO 
headquarters, welcomed members of the TAG-D group (1), thanking them for 
their continued commitment in supporting WHO as the group reaches its 
halfway term.  
 
She highlighted the many ways that TAG-D had contributed to WHO projects 
related to diabetes, including to the guidelines on pregnancy and diabetes and 
the therapeutic patient education curriculum for diabetes. She also

2 
 
acknowledged members’ engagement in high-level meetings, including as a 
speaker and a co-chair for WHO side events at the 2025 International Diabetes 
Federation (IDF) Congress.  
 
Ms Sanjana Marpadga, Technical Officer, Department of NCDs, Rehabilitation 
and Disability at WHO headquarters, updated the members on the progress of 
the GDC in each of its six workstreams (2). 
 
Workstream 1: Access to essential medicines and associated 
health technologies 
 
The GDC highlighted the ongoing pilot of the NCD Medicines Forecasting and 
Quantification tool, including a workshop in Ethiopia to test the trial version and 
gather feedback ahead of its pilot in 2025. Work is also being done on a technical 
manual to provide users with an overview of forecasting principles and guidance.  
 
In addition, the GDC reviewed applications for the 25th Expert Committee on 
Selection and Use of Essential Medicines (3). There were three applications 
related to diabetes submitted. Final decisions are expected soon. 
 
Workstream 2: Technical products 
 
Technical products are norms or standards, data and research that are 
applicable to multiple countries and developed using rigorous processes at 
global, regional and country levels to drive impact (4). Since the last TAG-D 
meeting in December 2024, four technical products related to diabetes have 
been published: 
 
• WHO operational handbook on tuberculosis and comorbidities (5), which 
includes a section on diabetes, was launched at the 2025 IDF Congress. 
• Mpox and diabetes: a needed public health research agenda (6) was published 
in The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology in collaboration with the WHO 
Health Emergencies Programme. Evidence suggests that people living with 
diabetes face higher risks of severe outcomes from mpox infection. 
• The double threat of diabetes and antimicrobial resistance (7), published in 
The Lancet Diabetes & Endocrinology, examined diabetes and antimicrobial 
resistance. This study showed that people with diabetes face nearly 
double the risk of antimicrobial resistance due to immune dysfunction, 
hyperglycaemia, and frequent exposure to broad-spectrum antibiotics.  
• Assessing national capacity for the prevention and control of 
noncommunicable diseases: report on 2023 global survey was published (8). 
The report showed that 53% of countries noted having a diabetes registry

3 
 
(increased by 6% since 2021) and that 92% of countries reported having 
guidelines for diabetes.  
 
The upcoming second Global status report on diabetes was also discussed. It will 
present a picture of the increasingly urgent global diabetes crisis and the 
persistent gaps in care to address it. The proposed chapters and themes were 
shared.  
 
Workstream 3: Prevention, health promotion and health literacy 
 
On World Obesity Day in March 2025, WHO launched the WHO Technical Package 
to Stop Obesity (9). This package provides a practical how-to approach to proven 
interventions selected by countries according to their priorities and tailored to a 
local context.  
 
Workstream 4: Country support 
 
The GDC updated on recent WHO regional and country support efforts. 
 
The WHO Regional Office for Europe worked with 13 volunteer countries to 
determine how the global diabetes targets could be measured within different 
country contexts. Eight case studies were completed on Armenia (10), Belgium 
(11), England (United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland) (12), Finland 
(13), Germany (14), Italy (15), Kyrgyzstan (16) and Tajikistan (17). There is an 
additional document that summarizes the key learnings from the process (18). 
 
The findings showed that while some countries had already achieved progress 
towards certain targets, overall performance remained below the global targets. 
The process demonstrated the feasibility of measuring the global diabetes 
targets using diverse approaches and data sources. It also highlighted the 
opportunity to expand such assessments to other regions and countries. 
 
Workstream 5: Research and innovation 
 
In a partnership with the Alliance for Health Policy and Systems Research, more 
implementation research for integrated diabetes management will be supported 
(19). There are projects planned in eight countries: Ghana, Ethiopia, India, Nepal, 
Morocco, Indonesia, Sri Lanka and Cambodia. These projects will drive 
improvements in diabetes care through implementation research that supports 
evidence-based integration of services in national health systems.

4 
 
There is also ongoing development of AI-based technology for the early detection 
of diabetic retinopathy. This includes setting a baseline indicator for retina 
screening coverage for people with diabetes and the development of AI 
standards for diabetic retinopathy screening.  
 
Workstream 6: Governance, strategy and partnership 
 
The GDC shared milestones about core stakeholder engagement activities, 
including the 5th Engagement event for people with lived experience of diabetes 
and the 8th Global Diabetes Compact Forum, which both took place in April 2025. 
The Forum meeting was co-hosted by Beyond T1D and convened more than 150 
non-State actors across all six WHO regions. 
 
In addition, the GDC hosted multiple events that increased visibility on the global 
stage. At the IDF World Diabetes Congress 2025, held in Thailand in April, the 
GDC co-hosted two side events with IDF: a 4th anniversary celebration featuring 
TAG-D Chair, Dr Nikhil Tandon, as a panelist, and the launch of the WHO 
operational handbook on tuberculosis and comorbidities (5), co-chaired by TAG-D 
member, Dr Bishwajit Bhowmik. 
 
At the 2025 World Health Assembly, the GDC was very active. There were 
multiple events, including: 
 
• Digital health transformation in the global NCD response side event co-
hosted by IDF and World Diabetes Foundation (22). 
• Unlocking access to insulin: Advancing public-private partnerships for 
equitable diabetes care side event co-hosted by the Helmsley Charitable 
Trust and Access to Medicine Foundation (23). 
• A social media live on NCDs (24). 
• A diabetes stakeholder discussion on leveraging the Fourth UN High-level 
Meeting on NCDs and Mental Health in 2025.

5 
 
Session 2: Development of WHO 
recommendations on the management of 
dyslipidaemia for primary and secondary 
prevention of cardiovascular disease 
 
Dr Kouamivi Agboyibor, Technical Lead for Cardiovascular Diseases, Department 
of NCDs, Rehabilitation and Disability at WHO headquarters presented an 
overview of WHO’s ongoing guideline development on recommendations for the 
management of dyslipidaemia. 
 
Dr Agboyibor explained dyslipidaemia and why its management is important for 
prevention of cardiovascular disease (CVD). He gave an overview of the guideline 
process to date, including scoping of non-WHO guidelines from various countries 
and expert organizations. There is currently no globally standardized WHO 
guidance for comprehensive lipid management for primary and secondary CVD 
prevention.  
 
He presented proposed PICO questions for this guideline development and 
asked TAG-D for feedback on the following questions:  
 
1. Are the target populations and eligibility criteria well-defined and 
appropriate for primary and secondary prevention? 
2. Are there any key subgroups or populations that should be added or 
prioritized? 
3. Are the interventions and comparators clearly articulated, and do they 
reflect the range of lipid-lowering therapies used in practice? 
4. Are the outcomes identified clinically relevant and aligned with patient 
and health system priorities? 
5. Are there any missing PICO questions or additional clinical questions that 
should be included, especially in relation to diabetes and cardiovascular 
risk? 
 
Discussion highlights 
 
TAG-D members raised several key considerations: 
 
• Risk assessment 
There was much discussion about risk calculators, which TAG-D members 
noted often underreport risk and may lead to the under-use of statins. 
They asked if countries could opt to use locally developed risk calculators

6 
 
or if WHO provide a standard. There may also be barriers to using risk 
calculators in the field, so having a digital tool and automated risk 
assessment may be helpful.  In response, Dr Agboyibor emphasized that 
the updated WHO 10-year risk calculator has been revised to account for 
multiple factors, including racial and ethnic differences. In addition, WHO 
has released a digital version of the risk calculator (https://cvd-
risk.smrwho.org/), which can be easily adopted and applied by countries to 
support implementation at the primary health care level. 
 
• Lipid testing vs statins prescribed as standard 
Given the cost and feasibility challenges of lipid testing in many low- and 
middle-income countries (LMICs), TAG-D discussed whether statins should 
be prescribed for all people with diabetes aged over 40, without requiring 
a lipid test. Even in people who are at low-risk, members suggested 
looking at family history, ethnicity and comorbidities. Some members 
proposed initiating statins in anyone with diabetes who has any other CVD 
risk factor, regardless of age or type of diabetes. In response, Dr Agboyibor 
informed that prescribing statins for all people with diabetes aged over 40 
is already an existing WHO guidance. The pending question is whether 
lipid assessment is necessary for setting treatment goals. The evidence 
from the ongoing systematic reviews will help guide the GDG members in 
developing clear recommendations. 
 
• Type 1 diabetes 
The TAG-D discussed whether special consideration was needed for 
people living with type 1 diabetes, as they are often diagnosed with 
diabetes at a younger age and may need a statin prescribed earlier. 
Therefore, long and early-onset type 1 diabetes can be considered as a 
subgroup. 
 
• Add-ons to statins 
The evidence is well-established for statins as first line and TAG-D 
members noted that most new therapies were studied as add-ons to 
statins. Given this, some PICO questions could be reframed as those 
around add-ons to statins. However, the cost of newer therapies needs to 
be considered, as many are more expensive than statins. Dr Agboyibor 
underlined that while statins remain the cornerstone of lipid-lowering 
therapy, it is important to consider patients who are statin-intolerant or 
those with conditions such as familial hypercholesterolaemia or elevated 
lipoprotein(a), in whom statins alone may be insufficient. In such 
situations, add-on or alternative therapies, including ezetimibe, PCSK9 
inhibitors, bempedoic acid, or in selected cases icosapent ethyl, may be

7 
 
required. Any guidance will need to weigh clinical benefit against 
affordability and accessibility, particularly in low- and middle-income 
countries, where ezetimibe may represent the most feasible option. 
 
• Other considerations 
TAG-D suggested if it may be worth exploring young-onset diabetes as a  
subgroup, as well as those with various comorbidities such as HIV. There 
are also variations based on ethnicity that should be factored in.  
 
TAG-D members agreed to provide written feedback on this guideline to better 
assist in the development process, including prioritization of PICO questions. 
 
 
Session 3: Demand forecasting tool for NCD 
medicines and health technologies 
 
Mr Bashier Enoos, Technical Officer, Department of NCDs, Rehabilitation and 
Disability, presented the Demand forecasting tool for NCD medicines and health 
technologies.  
 
The standardized NCD demand forecasting tool aims to strengthen the capacity 
of NCD country programmes. The tool is designed to inform and guide planning, 
national medical product budgets and country procurement plans, including 
potential price negotiations with suppliers and manufacturers to maximize 
limited budgets. It also helps manufacturers to better adapt production 
schedules to more effectively meet future demand, empowers NCD stakeholders 
to make more targeted, evidence-based advocacy efforts to close resource gaps 
and prevents or minimizes stock imbalances.  
 
Mr Enoos walked the TAG-D through the process thus far, including one of the 
pilot countries, namely Ethiopia, a demonstration of the tool followed thereafter.  
 
He asked the TAG-D to comment on the following questions: 
 
1. What are among the barriers to the accurate and timely forecasting of 
diabetes products at the LMIC country level?  
2. What are some key health programme (e.g. guidelines) considerations 
users need to be aware of when forecasting for diabetes products?  
3. What are some of the pros and cons of excluding pre-loaded regimens in 
the tool? (e.g. insulin)

8 
 
4. Often unconstrained demand exceeds budget availability - do you have 
any recommendations on how to improve financing for diabetes 
treatment? 
 
Discussion highlights 
 
TAG-D members expressed strong interest and support for the tool. They asked 
whether essential supplies, such as glucose-testing strips and other consumables 
would be included, as this will be important for people living with diabetes. Mr 
Enoos clarified that these items could be incorporated if they are listed in a 
country’s national essential medicines/diagnostic lists, though data quality may 
limit accuracy. 
 
Members were interested in how artificial intelligence had been or could be used 
to better model elements such as scale up by analysing previous examples.  
 
Clarification was requested on the indicators that will guide equitable 
distribution, the intended users of the tool and the approach to validating its 
accuracy. Members also reflected on the opportunity this tool presents to better 
understand country-specific financial considerations and how it could potentially 
enhance access to essential treatment.  
 
Next steps for this project include data collection and piloting the tool in Ethiopia 
in 2025, followed by other WHO regions. TAG-D members agreed to provide 
written feedback on the tool, if required. 
 
 
Session 4: Technical report on diabetes and oral 
diseases 
 
Dr Sangeeta Singh, Consultant, the WHO Regional Office for the Western Pacific, 
presented an overview of the Technical report on diabetes and oral diseases, which 
is currently in its final review.  
 
The technical report explores the bidirectional relationship between diabetes 
and oral diseases. Evidence from systematic reviews was synthesized to assess: 
• how diabetes affects oral health; 
• how oral diseases impact glycaemic control; and 
• evidence-based oral health interventions for people with diabetes.

9 
 
The report highlights the bidirectional relationship between periodontal diseases 
and diabetes, with each condition influencing the other. The evidence 
demonstrates that periodontal therapy improved glycemic control, especially for 
people with type 2 diabetes. Thus, preventive oral care can support both 
management of diabetes and oral diseases. 
 
Dr Singh requested the TAG-D’s input on the following questions: 
 
1. Do TAG-D members have any feedback or suggestions to strengthen the 
technical report?  
2. How can national diabetes programmes and diabetes stakeholders play a 
more active role in integrating essential oral health care into diabetes 
management? 
3. What next steps would you recommend following the launch of this 
technical report, for academic institutions, diabetes experts, associations 
of people living with diabetes and Member States?  
 
Discussion highlights 
 
TAG-D members highlighted the need to integrate oral health into primary 
health care and universal health coverage. They considered how an evaluation of 
the mouth could be included as part of other regular screening of feet or 
kidneys. Members noted potential alignment with existing programmes such as 
oral cancer screening. 
 
Members highlighted the need for greater awareness among health 
professionals about the association between diabetes and oral diseases. This 
gap could be addressed by enhancing health professional education, 
incorporating oral health into national guidelines, and fostering collaboration 
between diabetes and oral health organizations. 
 
It was suggested that a report on the burden of this complication would be 
helpful, as it is not well-understood. In addition, referral pathways and an 
implementation guide would be helpful for increased uptake. The TAG-D also 
suggested that it would be good to raise awareness of this topic on World 
Diabetes Day and World Oral Health Day.  
 
There was also an interest in exploring how AI and other digital innovations 
could be used for oral screening and diagnosis in primary care settings.

10 
 
Closing this session, Dr Benoit Varenne, Dental Officer, Department of NCDs, 
Rehabilitation and Disability at WHO headquarters invited TAG-D members to 
provide written feedback to the technical report. 
  
 
Session 5: Establishing the baseline for the global 
diabetes coverage targets 
 
Dr Jennifer Manne-Goehler, Consultant, Department of NCDs, Rehabilitation and 
Disability at WHO headquarters, presented considerations for establishing the 
baseline for the global diabetes coverage targets (2).  
 
The objective is to establish a rigorous baseline evaluation of the Global 
Diabetes Compact target attainment for Targets 1-5 in all countries. Estimates 
must use a data source and method that can serve as a foundation to monitor 
and evaluate the scale-up of diabetes care to 2030. 
 
Dr Manne-Goehler outlined the planned process and asked the TAG-D members 
for input on the following questions: 
 
1. Are there concerns about the absence of subgroups from the plan for 
Targets 1-4?  
2. Should we also focus on T1D diagnosis for Target 1?  
3. If yes to #2, should we use diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) at diagnosis, the 
T1D Index or another indicator?  
4. We welcome inputs for the draft questions for Target 5 that will go to 
ministries of health.  
5. Other comments or recommendations on the estimation plan. 
 
Discussion highlights 
 
TAG-D members emphasized the importance of accounting for disparities by 
age, geography (urban/rural), sex, and socioeconomic status in the modelling. 
Country-specific subgroups should be included where feasible, particularly in 
settings with significant population variation. 
 
There are nuances in incorporating type 1 diabetes under Target 1. Members 
acknowledged challenges in distinguishing insulin use for T1D versus T2D in 
LMICs due to limited data systems. DKA at diagnosis was suggested as a 
potential proxy for insufficient early detection or care.

11 
 
Target 5 was acknowledged as complex and in need of further clarification. TAG-
D suggested that countries be invited to identify national data sources to include 
in the model, and that Ministries of Health be engaged in validating assumptions. 
 
Additional considerations for the modelling approach included: 
• Concerns about using 2022 as the baseline year, given limited data 
availability. A 5-year window was proposed to improve representativeness. 
• The importance of clearly communicating that the estimates are modelled, 
not reported, and outlining their limitations. 
• The risk that reliance on pooled data (e.g. from the Institute for Health 
Metrics and Evaluation) could disincentivize national data collection. 
Guidance should emphasize the continued importance of country-led data 
systems. 
• Using obesity as a covariate or proxy in the model may be an issue, given 
ethnic differences in BMI thresholds and inconsistent data availability 
across countries. 
• Recognition that existing sources, such as WHO’s STEPwise approach to 
NCD risk factor surveillance, have limitations, including self-reporting bias, 
and should be interpreted with caution. 
 
Members of TAG-D agreed to provide written feedback to further outline their 
perspectives on these questions. 
 
 
Closing remarks 
 
The rapporteur, Dr Yacoba Atiase, summarized the eighth TAG-D meeting, 
highlighting the engaging discussions and key takeaways from each day. 
 
In her closing remarks on behalf of the TAG-D Secretariat, Dr Bianca 
Hemmingsen expressed gratitude to the TAG-D chair and rapporteur for their 
valuable contributions and commended the TAG-D members for their 
engagement in these discussions. 
 
 
Next steps 
 
The ninth TAG-D meeting, which will be the final meeting for the current TAG-D 
cohort, is planned for 15-16 December 2025.

12

metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
viii
Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
Worldwide, more than 820 million people live with diabetes. Diabetes is among the top 10 causes of death 
and an increase in premature deaths in adults aged 30–69 years of 3% has been observed from 2000 to 
2019. Medical devices, including in vitro diagnostics, such as blood glucose meters, the associated strips 
and lancets, syringes, and laboratory equipment to measure blood glucose and glycated haemoglobin are 
an integral part of the management of diabetes. For instance, syringes are essential for the administration 
of insulin, and blood glucose monitoring both in health facilities and at home are a cornerstone of proper 
diabetes management. A blood glucose meter is required for the successful management of diabetes which 
in turn prevents acute and chronic diabetes related complications. However, studies have shown limited 
availability of medical devices, including in vitro diagnostics, in low and middle-income countries. The Lancet 
Commission on Diagnostics highlighted that access to diagnostics is an essential component of achieving 
Universal Health Coverage by 2030. The Commission highlights a diagnostic gap of 56% for diabetes which is 
estimated to have a larger impact on morbidity and mortality than lack of access to treatment. 
In recent years, actions to address the existing gaps in access to medicines and health technologies for 
diabetes management led to the launch of the Global Diabetes Compact by the World Health Organization. 
One of the aims within the Global Diabetes Compact is to improve access to diagnostics through workstream 
one, dedicated to essential diabetes medicines and associated health technologies. In addition, the global 
coverage targets for diabetes, include “100% of people with type 1 diabetes have access to affordable insulin 
and blood glucose self-monitoring”. In 2021, the World Health Assembly adopted Resolution 74.4, Reducing 
the burden of noncommunicable diseases through strengthening prevention and control of diabetes 
which calls on Member States to ensure “… better access to safe, affordable, effective and quality essential 
diagnostics, medicines, and other related health products”. Also, a project entitled “Improving access to 
Noncommunicable disease medicines and health technologies through improved assessment, regulation, 
prequalification, availability, price transparency, procurement and engagement with the private sector 2022-
2024” was funded by the Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation. This report is part of this project 
which includes two main components, a scoping review and an online survey to collect data on barriers, price 
and affordability of different medical devices. The overall aim of this report was to document the availability, 
price and affordability of glucometers, strips, lancets, syringes, blood pressure measurement devices, and 
facility testing for blood glucose, blood pressure and glycated haemoglobin.
The scoping review collected relevant available information on the price and availability of blood glucose 
meters and test strips. The price compilation aimed to gather relevant information previously collected by 
third parties. The online survey was sent to biomedical engineers and key opinion leaders to investigate issues 
of availability and price for blood glucose meters, test strips, lancets, syringes, blood pressure measurement 
devices, glycated haemoglobin analysers at the health facility and the price of tests and devices to individuals.
With regards to availability of these medical technologies data from the review and online survey highlight

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
third parties. The online survey was sent to biomedical engineers and key opinion leaders to investigate issues 
of availability and price for blood glucose meters, test strips, lancets, syringes, blood pressure measurement 
devices, glycated haemoglobin analysers at the health facility and the price of tests and devices to individuals.
With regards to availability of these medical technologies data from the review and online survey highlight 
a lack of availability of the necessary medical devices for the proper management of diabetes. In looking at 
prices one methodological challenge is that prices of medical devices for diabetes management are reported 
inconsistently across the literature. From the data collected on prices the main finding is the variability of 
prices for blood glucose meters and strips. Although not from a representative sample some manufacturers of 
blood glucose meters and strips were found to have more products present in different markets than others. 
In comparing the price of a blood glucose meter versus the strips the cost of the strips outweighs the cost of 
the actual meter. Prices for lancets and syringes were also variable although less than for meters and strips. 
The cost of equipment for measuring glycated haemoglobin and the consumables was high. Blood pressure 
measurement devices were also found to be relatively high priced and again variability of prices, overall, 
within countries and by brands was found. When tests were performed in health facilities in some countries 
these are provided for free. If not, the price can be significant especially for glycated haemoglobin tests which 
is a cornerstone of diabetes management. In looking at the affordability of these different components of 
diabetes management the least affordable elements are strips and glycated haemoglobin testing meaning 
that many people will not be able to afford key aspects of blood glucose monitoring either daily at home or 
for regular follow-up in facilities. 
Executive summary

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
ix
Executive summary 
In looking at the issue of availability, price, and affordability of medical devices there is the need to assess 
these factors from the perspective of the health system and the individual. The current market for blood 
glucose meters makes this unaffordable for people in low- and middle-income countries and new approaches 
to self-monitoring adapted to these contexts are needed. This might include the development of universal 
meters or strips. The data presented in this report highlights the wide range of prices for different medical 
devices and therefore price transparency is needed. The inclusion of blood glucose meters in World Health 
Organization prequalification, a service provided by the World Health Organization to assess the quality, 
safety and efficacy of medicinal products and devices, is an important step from a regulatory perspective, 
From the financing angle there is the need to clarify how medical devices can be included in Universal 
Health Care packages as the current prices are unaffordable for many people in low- and middle-income 
countries. Affordability of self-monitoring is linked to the recommended number of tests per day an individual 
need to successfully manage diabetes. Thus, the financial implications of the clinical guidance needs to be 
considered. To improve affordability, mechanisms need to be in place to improve the procurement and supply 
of medical devices especially ensuring linkages between the durable and consumable elements. Overall, there 
is a need to reconsider the element of affordability for any medical device for the management of diabetes 
where there is a durable and consumable element. In addition, any new technology which may eliminate the 
need for a consumable element will need to consider the ability to pay for people in low- and middle-income 
countries. Barriers identified in the literature comprise both international and national barriers. International 
challenges included high level barriers related to production and manufacturing, as well as accuracy and 
quality assurance issues. National barriers included issues within national health systems. Individual barriers 
include all those encountered by the individual while seeking care.
The Global Diabetes Compact, World Health Assembly Resolution 74.4, World Health Assembly Resolution 
76.5 all mention the issue of availability and affordability of medical devices. With the target of “100% of 
people with type 1 diabetes have access to affordable insulin and blood glucose self-monitoring” being far 
from a reality in many contexts the World Health Organization, Member States, and other partners now need 
to find ways to ensure improved availability and affordability of these key elements for the management of 
diabetes to ensure improved outcomes for the millions of people with this condition.

content:
2
Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
One of the five Noncommunicable diseases (NCD) prioritized by the World Health Organization (WHO) is 
diabetes. Worldwide, more than 820 million people live with diabetes  (1). Diabetes is among the top 10 causes 
of death and is the only major NCD for which an increase in premature deaths in adults aged 30–69 years of 
3% has been observed between 2000 and 2019  (2). Globally it is estimated that 9 million people have type 1 
diabetes  (3) and about 60 million people with type 2 diabetes use insulin  (4). The burden of diabetes-related 
morbidity and mortality in low- and middle-income countries (LMIC) is present and currently placing an 
extreme challenge on health systems, which are unable to provide insulin and the necessary medical devices 
for a variety of reasons. LMICs will also see the largest increase in the coming decades, thus requiring issues 
of access to medical devices to be addressed urgently. 
Syringes are an essential medical product for the administration of insulin and blood glucose monitoring both 
in health facilities and at home are a cornerstone of proper diabetes management. A blood glucose meter 
(BGM) is used to monitor and track blood glucose levels.  Whereas a rapid increase in technology has been 
seen in high income countries (HIC) for both the delivery of insulin and measurement of blood glucose, these 
innovations have not reached many in LMICs.
The World Health Organization (WHO) priority medical devices list (MDL) for management of cardiovascular 
diseases and diabetes  (5)  presents lists of all devices needed to diagnose and manage diabetes and its 
most common complications. The MDL list includes clinical interventions through levels of care and across 
the continuum of care. For example, this list states that HbA1c point of care testing should be available at 
Levels 2 and 3 of care, whereas blood pressure measurement devices (BPMD) and BGMs should be present 
at all levels of care. Particularly relevant to this report is the MDL inclusion of interventions for diabetes 
management including “Blood glucose management, including self-monitoring and insulin injections” which 
enumerates all medical devices needed for such intervention as follows: BGM, strips, syringes, needles, swabs, 
lancets and even the control solution to calibrate the BGM. Furthermore, all MDLs, including the one for 
cardiovascular diseases and diabetes, have been integrated in a database known as WHO Priority Medical 
Devices Information System (MeDevIS)  (6)  facilitating access to information of the devices. All medical 
devices in this publication are listed in MeDevIS. The WHO Model List of Essential In Vitro Diagnostics (EDL) 
provides a list of tests seen as essential for diabetes (7). These include blood glucose levels and haemoglobin 
A1c (HbA1c). For context with no laboratories, it recommends urine dipsticks and BGM for blood glucose 
levels and handheld analysers for HbA1c. When laboratories are available it recommends optical methods 
and automated chemistry analysers for blood glucose levels and immunoassays for HbA1c  (7).  From the 
wider perspective,

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
A1c (HbA1c). For context with no laboratories, it recommends urine dipsticks and BGM for blood glucose 
levels and handheld analysers for HbA1c. When laboratories are available it recommends optical methods 
and automated chemistry analysers for blood glucose levels and immunoassays for HbA1c  (7).  From the 
wider perspective, 
Studies have shown limited availability of different essential diagnostics in LMICs (8). The Lancet Commission 
on diagnostics (9) highlights that access to diagnostics is an essential component of achieving Universal 
Health Coverage (UHC) by 2030. For diabetes data provided by the Commission highlights a diagnostic 
gap of 56% which is estimated to have a larger impact on morbidity and mortality than lack of access to 
treatment. Lack of access to diagnostics is presented by the Commission as being caused by various factors 
including geographical access to diagnostics, socioeconomic factors of the population, lack of infrastructure, 
organizational factors within the health system and human resource capacity. Specifically for diabetes much 
attention has been paid to the issue of access to insulin (10,11), as well as health system barriers to access to 
care and diagnostics (12-15).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
3
1. Introduction
Responding to the increasing burden of diabetes around the world, the WHO has launched the Global Diabetes 
Compact. The WHO Global Diabetes Compact has the vision of reducing the risk of diabetes, and ensuring 
that all people who are diagnosed with diabetes have access to equitable, comprehensive, affordable and 
quality treatment and care  (16).  In order to achieve the objective, the Global Diabetes Compact has six 
workstreams. One of the workstreams is focused on improve access to essential diabetes medicine and 
associated heath technologies.  This aspect of improving access to diagnostics is also reflected in the World 
Health Assembly (WHA) Resolution 74.4 with WHO calling on Member States to ensure “… better access to 
safe, affordable, effective and quality essential diagnostics, medicines, and other related health products”  
(17). Another WHA Resolution 76.5 focused on diagnostics raised the overall issue of access to affordable 
and quality assured diagnostics with a specific mention on self-testing as well as the presence of diagnostics 
in facilities (18). These high-level commitments have translated into a variety of concrete measures aimed to 
improve access to medical devices for diabetes with for example: “Asks” being put forward to medical device 
companies (19); BGMs and HbA1c analysers being added to WHO Prequalification (20); the inclusion of BGMs 
in the EDL for personal use  (21); as well as Global coverage targets for diabetes, including “100% of people 
with type 1 diabetes have access to affordable insulin and blood glucose self-monitoring” (22).
To improve availability and access to medical devices, Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation 
(NORAD) funded a project entitled “Improving access to NCD medicines and health technologies through 
improved assessment, regulation, prequalification, availability, price transparency, procurement and 
engagement with the private sector 2022-2024”. This project is a part of the Global Diabetes Compact 
workstream one and this report is part of this overall project. 
The aim of this report is to present new data from a scoping review of the literature, a price compilation 
and an online survey carried out by WHO to highlight the challenges with regards to access to BGMs, BGM 
strips, lancets, insulin syringes, HbA1c analysers and reagents (for facilities), BPMD, facility blood glucose 
testing and facility HbA1c testing for the management of diabetes to propose recommendations to improve 
knowledge of this issue and concrete steps to improve access.

metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
6
Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
2.1 Scoping review
The overall aim of the scoping review was to systematically collect relevant available information on medical 
devices for diabetes management. The scoping review was carried out using Google Scholar (search engine 
for scientific literature) for materials published in English only from 1990 onwards. Google Scholar was chosen 
for simplicity since it is open access. The initial search included the following search terms: “medical devices 
pricing”, “medical devices diabetes pricing”, “medical devices diabetes cost”, “medical devices diabetes 
availability”, “glucometer pricing”, “glucometer cost” and “glucometer availability”. We included publications 
in all types of diabetes mellitus (Type 1, Type 2, and gestational diabetes). This was done for all country 
income groups. All abstracts were reviewed by two people. The objective of the scoping review was to 
identify the following information in the literature:
1. Barriers and challenges for accessing the technologies for the management of diabetes.
2. Availability, prices and costs associated to technology for diabetes management.
3. Viable methodologies including dissemination strategies for the surveys, historically used indicators and 
questionnaires.
4. Strategies, reimbursement mechanisms and programs of successful provision in LMICs.
Notes were compiled using Mendeley Reference Manager on the necessary data to be included in the 
review. The results of the scoping review present the overall context and barriers in parallel to price and 
affordability data.
2.2 Price compilation
Third parties were contacted by WHO and a request was made for price and availability data on BMG and 
test strips, the Foundation for Innovative and New Diagnostics (FIND) provided data on the prices of BGMs, 
lancets and strips for different countries.
2.3 Online Survey
Two online questionnaires were designed by WHO and sent to two different audiences: biomedical 
engineers and key opinion leaders to be completed. The questionnaires were disseminated using the WHO 
newsletter, posted on biomedical engineering networks, and sent to a purposive sample of experts. The 
online questionnaire for biomedical engineers investigated issues of availability and price for BGMs, test 
strips, lancets, syringes, BPMDs, HbA1c analysers for health facilities and while the one for opinion leaders 
focused on the price of medical devices and tests to individuals. Both questionnaires are included in Annex 
1 and Annex 2. 
During the data analysis as data was missing on the actual manufacturer of BGMs this information was added 
based on the model reported in the questionnaire. For exchange rates if purchase year was provided the 
average annual exchange rate for the year reported was used. If no year was provided the average exchange 
rate for 2023 was used. Exchange rates were obtained from https:/ /fxtop.com and prices were not adjusted 
for inflation. If a range of prices was given the average price was used. This was done except for price 
data reported by biomedical engineers for syringes and HbA1c analysers where the two values of the range 
provided are presented. For Costa Rica no currency was provided by the key opinion leader responding. 
Prices in Costa Rican Colons were not feasible with prices for a BGM less than US$ 1, so it was assumed that

for inflation. If a range of prices was given the average price was used. This was done except for price 
data reported by biomedical engineers for syringes and HbA1c analysers where the two values of the range

Prices in Costa Rican Colons were not feasible with prices for a BGM less than US$ 1, so it was assumed that 
prices were reported in US$. In presenting data by income group the World Bank grouping was used  (23).

2. Methods
In comparing the prices of BGM with testing strip, it was assumed that the price of the meter was amortized 
over 24 months and that individuals used 3 strips per day. This enabled the durable versus variable cost to 
be calculated. To calculate affordability from the key opinion leader data the median price was used with the 
following assumptions presented in Table 2.

metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
9
3. Results
3. Results
The results present the findings from the scoping review, data provided by FIND and the results of the online 
survey for both biomedical engineers and key opinion leaders.
3.1 Scoping review
Results from “search terms” were scanned for relevant titles. Abstracts of publications with relevant titles 
were scanned (71) and a subset of articles (48) was selected for full article review. Additional articles were 
identified via the references of the fully screened articles and were also included in the review (23). A summary 
of all relevant data identified in each publication is listed in Annex 3.
Among the publications listing the price of the BGMs and/or the strips, some listed exact prices, while other 
publications listed price ranges (24-27) or prices aggregated by region or income level (24,25,28). In contrast, 
some publications reported annual cost of BGMs and/or strips per person (12,24,29,30) rather than the price 
of the device itself. Other publications reported the cost of self-monitoring blood glucose (SMBG) including 
the combined cost of BGMs, lancets and strips. Annual cost of SMBG per person  (31-33) or per population  
(34) were also reported and one publication reported the cost of SMBG per patient during pregnancy  (35). 
Another publication reported the annual median of out-of-pocket expenditure on medicines and medical 
devices combined (26). 
The most common period to report cost and expenditure was annually; however, expenses were also reported 
in monthly and daily intervals. One publication reported monthly expenses on medical devices and monthly 
expenses on strips. Another publication reported SMBG monthly cost (36), and one publication reported the 
daily cost of strips  (28). An additional metric used across reviewed literature for expenditure on diabetes 
management included the percentage of income spent annually on diabetes care  (15) (consultation costs, 
travel, insulin and SMBG) and percentage of income spent annually on BGMs and strips (37). The cost of the 
fee associated with the service (blood glucose test) at the health facility  (27,38-44) was reported mainly in 
settings where SMBG is not available. One publication reported the annual cost of blood glucose testing (44).
The literature also reported calculations of annual cost of managing diabetes per person (30,32,33,38,45-47) 
or for whole populations  (30-32,34). Calculations across the literature disaggregate the cost of diabetes in 
direct and indirect costs. The cost of medical devices was usually included within direct costs of diabetes. 
However, it is not always specified across disaggregated data and on occasion this was grouped with other 
costs. Annual direct cost of diabetes per person (28,30,33,46,47) and per population (34,46) identified within 
this review included components labelled as any of the following: daily self-management and home healthcare; 
medical devices and home healthcare; treatment and monitoring; BGMs and strips; and medical supplies. 
Annual excess cost of diabetes per person (30,34) and per population (30,45,46) were also reported across 
the literature. One publication reported excess cost per patient during pregnancy (35), and one publication 
reported annual excess on medical devices specifically (45).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
medical devices and home healthcare; treatment and monitoring; BGMs and strips; and medical supplies. 
Annual excess cost of diabetes per person (30,34) and per population (30,45,46) were also reported across 
the literature. One publication reported excess cost per patient during pregnancy (35), and one publication 
reported annual excess on medical devices specifically (45). 
Many studies report across diverse settings that the cost of SMBG exceeds the cost of insulin  (12,24,28-
30,33,34,36,48,49).
3.1.1 Scoping review – Challenges and barriers to access to medical devices for the 
management of diabetes
Different barriers and challenges in the provision of diabetes care were commonly described in the literature. 
A total of 31 articles mention various barriers and challenges accessing diabetes care  (12,15,25,27,28,39-
42,48,50-67). These barriers impact people being diagnosed with diabetes, receiving treatment and being 
controlled for diabetes. The cascade of care presented in Figure 2 shows that of all people with diabetes, only 
63.4% have ever had a blood glucose test, 44.3% were aware of having diabetes, 38.4% of people were on 
some form of treatment, and 22.8% were defined as having controlled diabetes (HbA1c < 8.0% or equivalent) 
(68). These data highlight the overall barrier of diagnosis and management of diabetes in LMICs due to lack 
of access to medical devices as well as weak health systems for the management of diabetes in LMICs.

content:
12
Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
3.1.2 Scoping review – Availability of medical devices for the management of 
diabetes
Throughout the literature availability was reported in different scales (regional, national, facility, and 
individual) and with different metrics. Availability was mostly presented as percentages, but on occasions it 
was reported using a qualitative scale (e.g., mostly available, rarely available, never available). Previous WHO 
publications reported availability of BGMs (along with other essential health technologies) by WHO Region 
and by income level (69). Different levels of availability use different metrics. For instance, at regional level, it 
is the percentage of countries with the technology available; at a national level, it is a percentage of facilities 
with the technology available; at the patient level is the proportion of consultations when the technology 
was available. In WHO service availability and readiness assessment (SARA), service availability refers to the 
physical presence of the delivery of services, encompassing health infrastructure, core health personnel, and 
service utilization.
3.1.3 Scoping review – Price of BGMs
Prices of medical devices for diabetes management were reported inconsistently across the literature and 
brands and models were never mentioned. Therefore, comparing prices across publications is often not 
possible. Prices were mostly reported in US Dollars, but some publications included cost in local currencies. 
Beside the price of the device, in settings where SMBG is not commonly available the patient fee for facility-
based testing was reported. Other commonly used metrics included the cost of diabetes for patients and 
country populations. Most cost calculations included patients’ direct cost (e.g., medicines, consultation, 
hospitalization) and indirect cost (e.g., transportation cost, days of work lost). In some calculations the 
expenses of people with diabetes were often compared against people without diabetes to estimate the 
excess cost of diabetes. In some studies models using secondary data were created to estimate countries’ 
annual expenditure in diabetes. Public data was also used to estimate national diabetes expenditure. 
From the review of the literature eight publications reported the unit price for a BGM for seven countries  
(28,35,39-41,53,54,70).  The prices reported ranged from US$ 9.00 to US$ 131.3.

3.3 Online questionnaire
The results of the online questionnaire are presented separately for biomedical engineers and key 
opinion leaders. 
3.3.1 Online questionnaire – biomedical engineers
For the online questionnaire a total of 120 records were submitted online by biomedical engineers. Of these 73 
records contained no information. This left 47 records with some data to be analysed. From these responses, 
one respondent stated they could not provide any information). Therefore, the overall analysis of the results 
for biomedical engineers was based on 46 records with many questions not or only partially answered. 
Thirty-nine respondents provided their role/position. Of these, 31 were biomedical engineers in different 
roles; 6 mentioned senior management roles (e.g., Medical Director); 1 nurse and 1 student. The description of 
the respondents can be found in Annex 4.
With regards to providing procurement and pricing information different number of biomedical engineers 
stated they were able to provide this information (Figure 6). It should be noted that not all those who stated 
they could provide this information did.

3.3.1.4 Biomedical engineers – Price of BGMs versus strips
As part of the analysis a comparison was done for the cost of the meter versus the cost of the strips as 
provided by biomedical engineers.  In most cases, except for Life Scan the monthly cost of strips far outweighs 
the cost of the actual meter. (Figure 10)
Fig. 10. Monthly cost of Blood Glucose Meter versus Strips (assuming 3 strips per day) from data 
reported by biomedical engineers and ratio of price of strips to glucose meters
3.3.1.5 Biomedical engineers – Price of lancets
There was a dearth of data reported on lancets. The price of one type of lancet in Nigeria was US$ 0.03. One 
respondent stated there is instability of prices.
3.3.1.6 Biomedical engineers – Price of HbA1c analysers
Only one biomedical engineer provided data for HbA1c analysers and their consumables and provided a 
range of prices for the machine (US$ 64.07-US$ 75.87) and for the consumables (US$ 67.44 -US$ 79.24).
3.3.1.7 Biomedical engineers – Price of BPMDs
The data provided by biomedical engineers on pack size and quantity purchased was often missing or hard to 
interpret. Therefore, only manufacturer, brand and price data were analysed. Respondents from five countries 
(Botswana, Ecuador, Kenya, and Nigeria) provided some information on manufacturers, brands, and prices of 
BPMDs. Ecuador provided all data except for the currency. This resulted in 13 data points for BPMDs. Of these 
6 were for different Omron machines and the 8 remaining for various other brands.
The results from biomedical engineers on using these 13 prices from 3 countries and 7 manufacturers are 
presented in Figure 11. A wide range can be seen in the prices for BPMDs overall and within countries. The 
highest priced BPMDs was 14 times more expensive than the lowest priced.

3. Results
3. Results
3.3.2 Online questionnaire – key opinion leaders
A total of 154 records were submitted online by key opinion leaders. Of these 95 records contained no 
information. This left 65 records with some data to be analysed. Details of the respondents can be 
found in Annex 5.
Fifty-six respondents provided information as to the type of organization they worked at. Most respondents 
reported working for Nongovernmental organizations (Figure 13).
Fig. 13. Distribution of types of organizations respondents reported working at (N= 65)
In the “Other” category this included people working at: hospitals; start-ups; a student; private sector; health 
insurance organization; and a diabetes association. Those reporting working for a governmental institution 
were either at the Ministry of Health or National Regulatory Agency. Thirty-seven respondents despite saying 
they would provide information on prices did not do this. Thus, the overall analysis of the results for key 
opinion leaders will be on 19 records with many questions not or partially answered.
3.3.2.1 Key opinion leaders – availability of BGMS for individuals
The data provided by key opinion leaders on pack size, facility type was often missing or hard to interpret. 
Therefore, only manufacturer, brand and price data were analysed. Respondents from 12 countries (Cameroon, 
Canada, Costa Rica, Cyprus, Eswatini, Guinea, India, Iraq, Latvia, Malta, Nigeria, South Africa, and Sweden) 
provided some information on Manufacturers, Brands, and prices of BGMs. Forty-five different manufacturers 
of BGMs were reported as presented in Fig.14.

In terms of brands, for those manufacturers with more than one mention, all the models from: Roche were 
Accu-Chek (different models reported); Life Scan One Touch (different models reported); Vivia Chek Ino and 
Smart were reported; and ACON On Call (different models reported). For Alere and Codefree no clear models 
were reported. 
3.3.2.2 Key opinion leaders – Price of BGMs for individuals
For price data 45 data points were available for analysis from 12 countries and 16 manufacturers. A wide range 
can be seen in the prices for BGMs overall, within countries, within country income groups and even within 
brands. The highest price reported was in Costa Rica (US$ 80). It is important to note that the respondent 
from Costa Rica did not specified currency therefore USD were assumed. Looking at country income groups 
the median price was highest in lower middle-income countries (US$ 30.68), followed by upper middle-
income countries (US$ 26.00) and finally high-income countries (US$ 25.40).
The respondent from Malta did not report any price data for BGMs but stated in the “Comment” section: “In 
the NHS (public service), BGMs are provided for free to all [people with diabetes]. Procurement for BGMs 
and compatible test strips is through a tendering system, and the brand and model may change according to 
chosen offers”. A similar scheme should be available in South Africa since a price of US$ 0.00 was reported. 
It is interesting to note that in both India and Sweden one source where people bought their BGMs, and Strips 
was in online stores.

Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
For price data 37 data points were available. As with BGMs a wide range of prices is found overall, by country 
and by brand (see Fig. 16). The highest priced test strip is 14 times more costly than the lowest priced strip and 
median prices in lower middle income countries (US$0.26) are similar to those from high income countries 
(US$0.28), but lower than what is reported from upper middle income countries (US$0.37). 
Fig. 16. Minimum, maximum, and median prices for strips for Blood Glucose Meters as reported 
by key opinion leaders
Similar to BGMs the findings for strips for BGMs shows high variability as well as some manufacturers being 
more frequently reported on in terms of price of strips. Data from key opinion leaders found that in all cases 
the cost of strips far outweighed the cost of the actual meter. The overall data here clearly highlights that the 
cost element of the strips is higher than that of the BGM (see Fig.17).

3.3.2.7 Key opinion leaders – Affordability of medical devices for the management of diabetes
In looking at the affordability based on data provided by key opinion leaders for the different medical 
technologies and tests as measured in number of days needed to work to afford 1 month of supplies or tests, 
in many countries the suggested threshold of 1 day’s wage to afford 1-month of supplies is not met. ( Figure 
19 and Table 14)  
Also, in looking at the cost of durable items, e.g., BGMs and BPMDs, these may be affordable if their lifetime 
cost is looked at versus people on low wages needing to disburse the overall cost of the medical technology 
at the time of its purchase.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
31
With regards to availability of medical devices data from the review and online survey highlight a lack of 
availability of the necessary medical devices for the proper management of diabetes. In looking at prices 
one methodological challenge is that prices of medical devices for diabetes management are reported 
inconsistently across the literature. From the data collected on prices the main finding is the variability of 
prices for blood glucose meters and strips.  Although not from a representative sample, some manufacturers 
of BGMs and strips are more common than others. In comparing the price of a meter versus the strips the cost 
of the strips outweighs the cost of the actual meter. Prices for lancets and syringes were also variable although 
less than for BGMs and strips. The cost of for measuring HbA1c were also expensive as were the consumables. 
BPMDs were also found to be relatively high priced and again variability of prices, overall, within countries 
and by brands was found. When tests are performed in health facilities in some countries these are provided 
for free. If not, the price can be significant especially for HbA1c tests which is a cornerstone of diabetes 
management. In looking at the affordability of these different components of diabetes management the least 
affordable elements are strips and HbA1c testing meaning that many people will not be able to afford key 
aspects of blood glucose monitoring either daily at home or for regular follow-up in facilities.
Barriers identified in the literature comprise both international and national barriers. International challenges 
included high level barriers related to production and manufacturing, as well as accuracy and quality assurance 
issues. National barriers included issues within national health systems. Individual barriers include all those 
encountered by the individual while seeking care.
The data collected for this report sheds light on the challenges related to the availability, pricing, and 
affordability of medical devices necessary for diabetes management. However, it is evident that we need 
more comprehensive data to gain a deeper understanding of the landscape surrounding these devices. While 
the presented information provides valuable insights, gaps remain, calling for further research. The used 
methodology faced challenges, particularly due to low response rates in the survey. To enhance future data 
collection, we must address these limitations by improving survey design or exploring alternative methods for 
collecting price data. The inconsistent reporting of prices across the literature poses another methodological 
challenge, hindering accurate comparisons. Additional data is crucial, especially regarding differences 
in availability, pricing, and affordability of medical devices used both within healthcare facilities and by 
individuals managing diabetes at home. Furthermore, investigating overall affordability and the cost of the 
complete package of medicines, medical devices, and services needed for diabetes management is essential.
In trying to address the range and diversity of issues with regards to the availability, price, and affordability 
of different medical devices for diabetes a holistic approach is needed. A framework developed by WHO 
for antibiotics (71) and applied to insulin  (11) can be useful to guide the next steps needed to address the 
challenge of medical devices (Figure 20). As the focus of this report was on availability, price and affordability 
some of the barriers beyond this need to be explored, for example issues surround intellectual property (72) 
as well as how medical devices are covered within the overall diabetes package within UHC to how these are 
delivered and supplied within the health system (12).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
challenge of medical devices (Figure 20). As the focus of this report was on availability, price and affordability 
some of the barriers beyond this need to be explored, for example issues surround intellectual property (72) 
as well as how medical devices are covered within the overall diabetes package within UHC to how these are 
delivered and supplied within the health system (12). 
In looking at Research & Development and Innovation two issues exist that need to be addressed. Firstly, 
intellectual property that might act as a barrier to improving access  (72). The next element is whether the 
innovations being developed are adapted to LMIC contexts  (73,74). These two factors are intimately linked 
to the business model of the manufacturers of medical devices. Here there needs to be a distinction between 
BGMs, strips, BP machines, HbA1c analysers and lancets and syringes. For BGM and strip manufacturers the 
element that generates the most income is the strip  (53). Lancets and syringes are disposable elements 
whereas the durable BGM and BP machine are different. The difference between a BP machine and a BGM is 
the issue of possibly “programmed obsolescence” with meters continually changing thus requiring new strips. 
In contrast BP machines can be seen as more durable in that they do not require a consumable component. 
HbA1c analysers might be in-between in that there might be less change in the models, but consumables/
reagents are needed. A certain dominance in the global market of some manufacturers of different medical 
devices is present and thus competition needs to be fostered. By increasing competition regulatory bodies 
need to ensure safe and quality assured products. A report by the Addressing the Challenge and Constraints 
of Insulin Sources and Supply Study identified significant weaknesses in the regulatory pathways for BGMs  
(75). This key step in promoting access to medical devices is needed to inform selection, pricing, and 
reimbursement. 
Discussion

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
32
Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes
Currently in most LMICs the costs of medical technologies and laboratory tests in health facilities are paid out 
of pocket by individuals (12,48). Pricing of medical devices is relatively opaque and in many cases these need 
to be bought in the private sector due to little or no availability in the public sector (27,38,42,43,55). This poor 
availability is also due to procurement and supply. Whereas insulin companies have developed differential 
pricing mechanisms no such initiatives exist for medical devices. It has been found that supplies of laboratory 
equipment and consumables in health systems is often poor  (14,27,38,39,42,43,55). Also for syringes it was 
found that there was a disconnect between the quantity of insulin ordered and syringes  (39). Procurement 
and supply need to think of the full diabetes package needed both for health facilities and individuals. The 
final step of prescribing, dispensing and use of medical devices is relatively under documented in LMICs. 
Guidelines for SMBG are needed and these then need to be in line with what is prescribed either for home use 
or within facilities. As the model used was developed for medicines a key stakeholder missing in this model 
is the laboratory technician or biomedical engineers. For facility-based testing there is the need for adapted 
medical technologies to the context and level of the health system for laboratory tests. For optimal use of 
medical technologies for SMBG and general diabetes management there is the need for education as even 
in donation programs where many of the medical devices and laboratory tests are provided for free poor 
outcomes are still being seen (76,77). Also, guidelines need to consider the economic reality where lancets or 
syringes might be used more than they should (78), as well how to best use a limited number of strips.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign 20.4 (Macintosh)
metadata.author: WHO
metadata.title: Availability, price and affordability of health technologies for the management of diabetes

content:
33
Discussion
In looking at the issue of availability, price, and affordability of medical devices there is the need to assess 
these factors from the perspective of the health system and the individual. The current market for BGMs makes 
this unaffordable for people in LMICs and new approaches to self-monitoring adapted to LMICs are needed. 
This might include the development of non-propietary universal meters or strips. The data presented in this 
report highlights the wide range of prices for different medical devices and therefore price transparency is 
needed. The inclusion of BGMs in WHO prequalification is an important step, but more is needed to ensure 
that products being used are quality assured from the appropriate regulatory agencies. From the financing 
angle there is the need to see how medical devices can be included in UHC packages as at current prices 
these are unaffordable for many people in LMICs. This aspect needs to include a link with guidelines to ensure 
coherence between the clinical aspects and their financial implications for the health system and individuals. 
To improve affordability, mechanisms need to be put in place to improve the procurement and supply of 
medical devices especially ensuring linkages between the durable and consumable elements. Overall, there 
is a need to reconsider the element of affordability for any medical device for the management of diabetes 
where there is a durable and consumable element. In addition, any new technology which may eliminate the 
need for a consumable element will need to consider the ability to pay for people in LMICs. 
The Global Diabetes Compact, WHA Resolution 74.4, WHA Resolution 76.5 all mention the issue of availability 
and affordability of medical devices. With the target of “100% of people with type 1 diabetes have access 
to affordable insulin and blood glucose self-monitoring” being far from a reality in many contexts the WHO, 
Member States, and other partners now need to find ways to ensure improved availability and affordability of 
these key elements for the management of diabetes to ensure improved outcomes for the millions of people 
with this condition.

Klatman 2020 41 coun-
